author: @VeniceInventors
text:
Steampunk robots are more endearing (or scary) than shiny white plastic ones! That reminds me of the Daleks. They have that steampunk look too!
Adhesive copper tape works well on smooth surfaces, at first it's very shiny and pink, but after a few weeks it has that nice brownish look. 
I painstakingly covered all the red paint on my bike with it, and now it looks like a bike made out of copper pipes from the 1800's 🙂

author: @PhiloSage
text: I have done that several times, but they don't work well on contact surfaces. Two things that I tried to prolong the coating is to use acetone vapor to soften the surface then coat the plastic, but the wax and plastic don't really like each other. The second is to anneel the plastic with the coating, but it really doesn't work like I would prefer. The best that I've found is to acetone vapor the part then spray metal powder across the surface, and then anneel and it looks very good. Using lubricating metals such as brass/bronze do have a significant reduction in friction.

author: @ThatRobHuman
text:
I've used Rub'n'buff (which is basically what those are) quite a number of times on my prints. It looks like you already do, but just in case folks actually read the comments: a Matte black PLA is your best bet to get full mileage out of Rub'n'buff.

Purely for aesthetic purposes another thing I''ve taken to doing that takes little effort is to cut circular grooves (say, 3mm deep) into my round parts (kind of like I gave them a trepanning treatement) and print a separate ring in another color (sometimes with a fancy-texture build plate) and just glue the ring into the groove. It makes little difference to the function of the part, but its pretty low effort breaks up a lot of a design. For example a deep purple silk PLA ring glued into a grove of that brass-treated ratchet wheel (maybe even slightly recessed?) might make it even more visually interesting.

author: @burntimeUK
text:
graphite powder works well for an easy metallic finish too, though you would need to prime the prints first.

check out the cosplayer/prop makers 'weathering' tips for some great methods of using metallic waxes, they can make 3D prints look incredible.

author: @TomFarrell-p9z
text: I have a book on the history of building toys and they explained the popularity of red, yellow, and orange toy army tanks during World War II by saying all toy makers knew "bright primary colors appeal to very young children".  We were just starting to use a 3D printer to make models at work, so during a few briefings explaining our "toys", I added "and senior managers" to the line.  Always got a laugh and I never advanced from line engineer to manager myself, so a double success!  🙂

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: 100% primary colours are very attractive - love them myself - who doesn't?

replies.author: @sashatz3387
replies.text: What's the name of the book?

replies.author: @TomFarrell-p9z
replies.text: @sashatz3387 Toys Go To War, by Jack Matthews.  Specifically about toys in the US in 1940's.  I mis-remembered about building them--I also have several books on making wooden toys.

author: @helmimakes
text: I learned that to apply a darker color on top of the metallic. You then wipe as much of it as you can off again. That gives you a darker color in the corners and crevices of the model. Makes the metallic pop and makes 3d prints look older and somewhat used

author: @skelingtonrick
text: I know I have commented this before, but I wanna once again insist you try a quick finishing treatment of a 5-10 min epoxy. it gives a lovely glossy smooth finish and if its a thin coat it wont run or drip it just pulls a nice flat surface across all the layer lines. then applying these rub-n-buff wax finishes leave an even more convincing metal look. might make for a good short video at the very least

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text: An effective way of preventing white objects and objects assembled from parts which share a uniform colour, from looking washed out on video is to illuminate the scene or room with several light sources of differing colour temperatures or even bounce the light off sheets of colour paper etc.  The lighting set up doesn't need to be anything fancy in order to make the model’s shape very apparent as any table top or bench lights etc., in close proximity to the model or object will do.  The same objects may not look washed out to our eyes because they are sensitive enough to see ‘some’ reflected light on the model from other objects in the room.

author: @disklamer
text:
That's a nice way to make plastic look more interesting, it also looks kind of matte which I like, it's easy to make things shiny but that doesn't look as real IMO.
If you colour the edges first, that already gives a bit of depth, fade out to the flat areas. Also, more random looks more real :)
Now I can not unsee the pendulum looks like an octopus trying to catch the sun.
